data.source: World Values Survey (primarily the 2000 wave, with some observations from the 1995 wave) merged with cross-country macro data on government consumption and transfers/subsidies
data.wave_or_subset: WVS 2000 wave; limited use of WVS 1995 wave for countries lacking 2000 data
data.access_details: WVS data are publicly available after free registration; macro fiscal data compiled from publicly available international databases (exact sources not detailed)
data.notes: Polarization measures created from four economic policy questions; additional robustness datasets include press freedom, corruption indices, ethnic/religious/linguistic fractionalization and Gini inequality.

method.description: Cross-sectional country-level regressions relate political polarization (derived from survey responses) to government size measures while controlling for economic, demographic, and institutional factors, with separate analyses for strong and weak democracies.
steps: Obtain individual-level responses to four economic policy questions from the WVS.
Compute country-level polarization measures (standard deviation and alternative indices).
Merge polarization measures with macro indicators of government size (GOVCONS, GOVTRANSSUB) and control variables.
Classify countries as strong or weak democracies.
Estimate OLS regressions of government size on polarization for three control specifications (basic, short, long).

Conduct robustness checks with alternative polarization indices, additional controls (inequality, press freedom, corruption, voter turnout, legislative fragmentation), and income-adjusted polarization.
Interpret coefficients, focusing on sign, magnitude, and statistical significance, especially within strong democracies.
method.models: Ordinary Least Squares (linear regression) with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors
method.outcome_variable: Government consumption as a share of total consumption (GOVCONS); secondary outcome: government transfers and subsidies (GOVTRANSSUB)
method.independent_variables: Political polarization measures based on survey dispersion (e.g., SD_EQUALITY, SD_PRIVATE, SD_GOV, SD_COMP)
method.control_variables: GDP per capita, trade openness, demographics (population size, dependency ratios), colonial origin dummies, geographic region dummies, Gini inequality, ethnic/religious/linguistic fractionalization, press freedom, corruption perceptions, voter turnout, legislative and government fragmentation indices

results.summary: Across 74 countries, higher political polarization is associated with smaller government size, with the relationship driven by strong democracies. In strong democracies, a one-standard-deviation increase in polarization predicts roughly a 4-percentage-point reduction in government consumption. Coefficients remain negative and significant under multiple specifications and robustness checks; no significant relationship is found for weak or non-democracies.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Government consumption (GOVCONS) – strong democracies, long specification
numerical_results.unit: percentage points of total consumption per 1-SD increase in polarization (EQUALITY question)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Government consumption (GOVCONS) – strong democracies, pooled questions (average effect)
numerical_results.unit: percentage points of total consumption per 1-SD increase in polarization

metadata.original_paper_title: Political Polarization and the Size of Government
